name: Leet Code
overview:
An online judge accepts user-submitted code solutions, compiles and executes them in a sandboxed
environment, runs them against test cases, and returns results (Accepted, Wrong Answer, Time Limit
Exceeded, etc.). The system must securely isolate user code execution to prevent malicious code from
affecting other users or the host system, handle concurrent submissions efficiently, and return results within a
few seconds. The system must support multiple programming languages.

inScope: Submit code in Python, Java, C++, JavaScript
Execute against hidden test cases in a sandbox
Time limit: 2 seconds per test case

items: Support 10k concurrent submissions with results returned within a few seconds
Sandbox isolation must be strong — user code must never affect other users or the host system
Verdict accuracy: time and memory limits enforced precisely
99.9% availability; submission queue must not be lost on worker failure
Horizontally scalable: add worker nodes to handle submission spikes (contests)
Pre-warmed execution pools eliminate cold-start latency for common languages

entities.name: Problem
entities.schema:
{
  problem_id: int,
  title: string,
  description: string,
  difficulty: enum(EASY|MEDIUM|HARD),
  time_limit_ms: int,

  memory_limit_mb: int,
  test_cases: [{ input: string, expected_output: string }],
  starter_code: map<language, string>
}

entities.name: Submission
entities.schema:
{
  submission_id: UUID,
  problem_id: int,
  user_id: string,
  language: enum(PYTHON|JAVA|CPP|JS),
  code: string,
  status: enum(PENDING|RUNNING|ACCEPTED|WRONG_ANSWER|TLE|MLE|RUNTIME_ERROR),
  runtime_ms: int,
  memory_mb: int,
  submitted_at: timestamp
}

approaches.title: Execute Directly on Host
approaches.description: Run user code directly on the server without isolation. Catastrophically insecure. User code can read/write files, make network calls, fork-bomb the server, or access other users' data.

approaches.title: Docker Containers
approaches.description: Run each submission in a Docker container with resource limits (CPU, memory, network disabled). Docker provides good isolation but container startup time (~500ms) adds latency. Container escape vulnerabilities exist (though rare).
approaches.tradeoff: Container startup latency; container escape risk; Docker daemon is a privileged process.

approaches.title: gVisor / Firecracker MicroVMs
approaches.description: Use gVisor (Google's user-space kernel) or Firecracker microVMs for stronger isolation. gVisor intercepts all system calls and runs them in a sandboxed kernel. Firecracker microVMs start in <125ms and provide VM-level isolation with minimal overhead. Worker nodes maintain a pool of pre-warmed execution environments to eliminate startup latency.
approaches.tradeoff: gVisor has higher syscall overhead (~2x); Firecracker requires KVM support on host.

approaches.title: Polling
approaches.description: Client polls GET /submissions/{id} every second until status changes. Polling adds unnecessary load. 10k concurrent submissions × 1 req/sec = 10k req/sec just for polling.

approaches.title: WebSocket Push
approaches.description: Execution worker publishes result to Kafka. A result delivery service consumes Kafka and pushes the result to the client via WebSocket. Client receives result within 100ms of execution completion. No polling overhead. WebSocket connection is maintained for the duration of the submission (typically <10 seconds).
approaches.tradeoff: WebSocket adds connection management complexity; requires sticky routing.

components.name: Submission API
components.responsibility: Accepts submissions; validates code size; publishes to Kafka; returns submission_id

components.name: Kafka
components.responsibility: Submission queue; partitioned by language for language-specific worker pools

components.name: Execution Workers
components.responsibility: Pull submissions from Kafka; spin up sandbox; execute code; publish results

components.name: Sandbox
components.responsibility: (gVisor/Firecracker) Isolated execution environment; enforces CPU/memory/time limits

components.name: Result Service
components.responsibility: Consumes execution results from Kafka; pushes to client via WebSocket

components.name: Submission DB
components.responsibility: (PostgreSQL) Stores submission records and results; indexed by user_id and problem_id

components.name: Problem DB (PostgreSQL)
components.responsibility: Stores problem definitions and test cases; read-heavy; cached in Redis

components.name: Leaderboard Service
components.responsibility: Computes rankings by acceptance rate and runtime; updated asynchronously

questions.title: 2. Test Case Execution
questions.content:
Each submission is run against all test cases sequentially. If any test case fails, execution stops and the verdict
is returned immediately (fail-fast). For TLE detection, the execution is wrapped in a timeout: if the process
doesn't complete within time_limit_ms, it receives SIGKILL. Memory usage is monitored via /proc/{pid}/status
and compared against memory_limit_mb.

questions.title: 3. Pre-Warmed Execution Pools
questions.content:
Cold-starting a Firecracker microVM takes ~125ms. To eliminate this latency, each worker node maintains a
pool of 10 pre-warmed microVMs per language. When a submission arrives, a pre-warmed VM is claimed from
the pool, the code is injected, and execution begins immediately. After execution, the VM is discarded (not
reused) to prevent state leakage. The pool is continuously replenished in the background.

questions.title: 4. Handling Concurrent Submissions
questions.content:
10k concurrent submissions require 10k execution environments. Worker nodes are horizontally scalable —
each node runs 10 concurrent executions (one per pre-warmed VM). 1,000 worker nodes handle 10k
concurrent submissions. Kafka consumer groups distribute submissions across worker nodes. Auto-scaling
adds worker nodes when Kafka consumer lag exceeds a threshold.

questions.title: 5. Leaderboard and Statistics
questions.content:
The leaderboard shows the fastest accepted solutions per problem. After each accepted submission, the
Leaderboard Service updates the problem's runtime distribution (stored as a histogram in Redis). The user's
submission is ranked by comparing its runtime against the distribution. This gives the 'Faster than X% of
submissions' metric shown on LeetCode.

keyFlows: ① Client submits code → Submit API → Kafka (partitioned by language)
② Exec Worker claims submission → fetches test cases from Problem DB
③ Worker spins up pre-warmed Sandbox → executes code → collects verdict
④ Result published to Kafka → Result Svc → WebSocket push to client
⑤ Accepted submissions update Leaderboard runtime distribution

whatToStudyFurther.content:
Expert Note: Study Linux namespaces and cgroups: how they provide process isolation (PID, network,
filesystem namespaces) and resource limits (CPU, memory, I/O) — the foundation of container technology.
Expert Note: Learn about gVisor's architecture: how the Sentry (user-space kernel) intercepts syscalls, the
Gofer process for filesystem access, and the performance trade-off vs. native execution.
Expert Note: Understand Firecracker's design: how it uses KVM to create lightweight microVMs, the jailer
process for additional isolation, and how it achieves <125ms boot time.
Expert Note: Study seccomp-bpf filters: how they use BPF programs to whitelist/blacklist syscalls, and how
to design a minimal syscall allowlist for a code execution sandbox.
Expert Note: Learn about the Judge0 open-source online judge: its architecture, how it handles multiple
languages, and how it compares to building a custom judge from scratch.

LeetCode (Online Judge)
